summary: Canadian artist, heavily inspired by the Indigenous peoples of British Columbia. Known for painting totem poles, forests, and landscapes of the Pacific Northwest. Associate of the Group of Seven (particularly Lawren Harris). Also a writer — her memoir Klee Wyck won the Governor General's Award. Became widely recognized only late in life (age 57). Went by the nickname "Klee Wyck" ("Laughing One").

works.name: Indian Church / Church at Yuquot Village (1929)
works.description: Her most reproduced painting. A white church surrounded by towering green forest. Renamed by the Art Gallery of Ontario in 2018. Wikipedia

images.caption: Indian Church / Church at Yuquot Village (1929)

works.name: Totem Pole Paintings (Big Raven, Big Eagle, Vanquished, etc.)
works.description: Carr's most iconic subject matter — totem poles in wilderness settings, often depicting Haida and other First Nations sites. Wikipedia

works.name: The Crazy Stair / The Crooked Staircase (1928)
works.description: Features a totem figure and a crooked staircase leading to a white building with a large bird on top. Sold for $3.39 million at auction. Wikipedia

works.name: Klee Wyck (1941 memoir)
works.description: Memoir of her experiences among First Nations peoples on BC's west coast. Won the Governor General's Award. "Klee Wyck" means "Laughing One" — a nickname given to Carr by the Ucluelet people. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Carr is identified and biographical clues.

recursive_suggestions: Group of Seven — frequently linked; Carr is an associate member
Lawren Harris — Carr's main supporter within the Group of Seven
Haida Gwaii — appeared as its own answerline (2025 Chicago Open) with Carr mention
